case_name: Tarachand Raosaheb Chaudhari vs The Oriental Insurance Co Ltd
court: MAHARASHTRA STATE CONSUMER DISPUTES REDRESSAL COMMISSION, MUMBAI, BENCH AT AURANGABAD
citation: FIRST APPEAL NO. : 619 OF 2022 IN COMPLAINT CASE NO.: 336 OF 2021
compensation_amount: Rs. 2,50,000/- with interest @ 8% p.a. from 06.05.2021 till realization; plus Rs. 10,000/- for mental agony, financial harassment and cost of litigation

compensation_breakdown.interest: 8% p.a. from 06.05.2021 (date of repudiation) till realization
compensation_breakdown.notes: Additionally, Rs. 10,000/- awarded for mental agony, financial harassment and cost of litigation.

facts: The appellant purchased a Corona Rakshak Policy from The Oriental Insurance Company Ltd. (Policy No. 182100/48/2021/4321) valid from 22.09.2020 to 03.07.2021 on payment of premium of Rs. 2,580/-. On 09.03.2021 she tested COVID-positive (RTPCR) and was admitted to MIT Boys Hostel, a COVID centre declared by the Aurangabad Municipal Corporation, from 09.03.2021 to 16.03.2021 and was listed as a COVID patient by VRDL Laboratory, Govt. Medical College, Aurangabad. She submitted a claim on 19.03.2021, but the insurer repudiated it on 06.05.2021 citing clause 3.6 of the policy. The District Consumer Commission dismissed her complaint, leading to this appeal.
arguments_claimant: The appellant contended that the District Commission failed to properly consider the policy terms, including clause 3.6, and the evidence. She argued that under the Corona Rakshak Policy, eligibility requires being COVID-positive and being hospitalized for at least 72 hours in a government hospital or a hospital set up under a local authority; she was hospitalized for 8 days in a COVID centre approved/declared by the Aurangabad Municipal Corporation. She asserted there was no breach of policy and repudiation was wrongful.
arguments_respondent: The insurer argued that the discharge card showed the treatment was simple and there was no need for admission, with no active line of treatment during hospitalization; therefore the claim was not admissible under exclusion clause 3.6. It also contended that the COVID centre was a quarantine centre and could not be treated as a hospital, hence there was no deficiency in service and no error in the District Commission’s dismissal.
ratio_decidendi: The Commission held that under the policy definition of hospital, “For the purpose of this policy any other set-up designated by the government as hospital for the treatment of Covid-19 shall also be considered as hospital” (clause 3.6(vi)). Since the appellant “was referred and admitted for treatment at MIT Boys Hostel which was declared as Covid centre by Aurangabad Municipal Corporation having medical practitioners and duly qualified nursing staff,” the COVID centre fell “within the purview of clause 3.6 (vi) of policy.” Therefore, “repudiation of claim on the said ground is deficiency in service on the part of respondent no.1,” because the appellant “fulfill the eligibility criteria required for the insurance benefit” by being COVID-positive and “hospitalized for more than 72 hours as per the advice of qualified medical practitioner.” On that basis, the District Commission’s dismissal was found erroneous for ignoring these facts and was quashed, and the insured was granted the lump-sum sum insured with interest.
final_order: Appeal partly allowed; the District Commission’s order in C.C.No.336/2021 dated 26.07.2022 was quashed and set aside. Respondent No.1 was directed to pay Rs. 2,50,000/- under the Corona Rakshak Policy with interest @ 8% p.a. from 06.05.2021 (date of repudiation) till realization, and Rs. 10,000/- for mental agony, financial harassment and cost of litigation; no order as to costs.
summary: This appeal concerned denial of a Corona Rakshak Policy claim after the insured tested COVID-positive and stayed for more than 72 hours in a municipal COVID care centre. The insurer refused payment saying the centre was not a hospital and the treatment was simple. The State Consumer Commission held that the policy itself treats a government-designated COVID set-up as a hospital, so repudiation was wrongful. The insurer was ordered to pay Rs. 2.5 lakh with 8% interest from the repudiation date plus Rs. 10,000 for harassment/litigation.
embedding_summary: Maharashtra State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission, Mumbai (Bench at Aurangabad), judgment dated 21.10.2024 in First Appeal No. 619 of 2022 (arising from District Consumer Commission Aurangabad C.C.No.336/2021 dated 26.07.2022): Jayashree w/o Satish Kharote v. The Oriental Insurance Company Ltd. & MD India Health Insurance TPA (ex parte). Insured purchased Corona Rakshak Policy No. 182100/48/2021/4321 valid 22.09.2020–03.07.2021; premium Rs. 2,580/-. COVID-positive by RTPCR on 09.03.2021; hospitalized at MIT Boys Hostel COVID centre (declared by Aurangabad Municipal Corporation) from 09.03.2021 to 16.03.2021; claim submitted 19.03.2021; repudiated on 06.05.2021 citing clause 3.6 (hospital definition/exclusion). Key issue: whether municipal COVID centre qualifies as “hospital” under policy and whether hospitalization exceeded 72 hours as required by clause 2 (operative clause) and clause 4.1 (COVID cover). Commission applied clause 3.6(vi): “any other set-up designated by the government as hospital for the treatment of Covid-19 shall also be considered as hospital,” held repudiation was deficiency in service, and quashed the District Commission dismissal. Final relief: insurer to pay lump sum Rs. 2,50,000/- with 8% p.a. interest from 06.05.2021 till realization plus Rs. 10,000/- for mental agony/harassment/litigation; no costs.
legal_principles: Insurance policy interpretation based on express definition of “hospital” including government-designated COVID-19 treatment set-ups (clause 3.6(vi))
Eligibility under Corona Rakshak Policy: COVID positive diagnosis from government-authorized diagnostic centre and minimum 72 hours continuous hospitalization (clauses 2 and 4.1)
Repudiation contrary to policy terms amounts to deficiency in service under consumer law
Quashing of District Commission order for failure to appreciate material evidence and policy clauses
bm25_text: Tarachand Raosaheb Chaudhari vs The Oriental Insurance Co Ltd MAHARASHTRA STATE CONSUMER DISPUTES REDRESSAL COMMISSION, MUMBAI, BENCH AT AURANGABAD 2024 The appellant purchased a Corona Rakshak Policy from The Oriental Insurance Company Ltd. (Policy No. 182100/48/2021/4321) valid from 22.09.2020 to 03.07.2021 on payment of premium of Rs. 2,580/-. On 09.03.2021 she tested COVID-positive (RTPCR) and was admitted to MIT Boys Hostel, a COVID centre declared by the Aurangabad Municipal Corporation, from 09.03.2021 to 16.03.2021 and was listed as a COVID patient by VRDL Laboratory, Govt. Medical College, Aurangabad. She submitted a claim on 19.03.2021, but the insurer repudiated it on 06.05.2021 citing clause 3.6 of the policy. The District Consumer Commission dismissed her complaint, leading to this appeal. The appellant contended that the District Commission failed to properly consider the policy terms, including clause 3.6, and the evidence. She argued that under the Corona Rakshak Policy, eligibility requires being COVID-positive and being hospitalized for at least 72 hours in a government hospital or a hospital set up under a local authority; she was hospitalized for 8 days in a COVID centre approved/declared by the Aurangabad Municipal Corporation. She asserted there was no breach of policy and repudiation was wrongful. The insurer argued that the discharge card showed the treatment was simple and there was no need for admission, with no active line of treatment during hospitalization; therefore the claim was not admissible under exclusion clause 3.6. It also contended that the COVID centre was a quarantine centre and could not be treated as a hospital, hence there was no deficiency in service and no error in the District Commission’s dismissal. The Commission held that under the policy definition of hospital, “For the purpose of this policy any other set-up designated by the government as hospital for the treatment of Covid-19 shall also be considered as hospital” (clause 3.6(vi)). Since the appellant “was referred and admitted for treatment at MIT Boys Hostel which was declared as Covid centre by Aurangabad Municipal Corporation having medical practitioners and duly qualified nursing staff,” the COVID centre fell “within the purview of clause 3.6 (vi) of policy.” Therefore, “repudiation of claim on the said ground is deficiency in service on the part of respondent no.1,” because the appellant “fulfill the eligibility criteria required for the insurance benefit” by being COVID-positive and “hospitalized for more than 72 hours as per the advice of qualified medical practitioner.” On that basis, the District Commission’s dismissal was found erroneous for ignoring these facts and was quashed, and the insured was granted the lump-sum sum insured with interest. Appeal partly allowed; the District Commission’s order in C.C.No.336/2021 dated 26.07.2022 was quashed and set aside. Respondent No.1 was directed to pay Rs. 2,50,000/- under the Corona Rakshak Policy with interest @ 8% p.a. from 06.05.2021 (date of repudiation) till realization, and Rs. 10,000/- for mental agony, financial harassment and cost of litigation; no order as to costs. This appeal concerned denial of a Corona Rakshak Policy claim after the insured tested COVID-positive and stayed for more than 72 hours in a municipal COVID care centre. The insurer refused payment saying the centre was not a hospital and the treatment was simple. The State Consumer Commission held that the policy itself treats a government-designated COVID set-up as a hospital, so repudiation was wrongful. The insurer was ordered to pay Rs. 2.5 lakh with 8% interest from the repudiation date plus Rs. 10,000 for harassment/litigation. Maharashtra State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission, Mumbai (Bench at Aurangabad), judgment dated 21.10.2024 in First Appeal No. 619 of 2022 (arising from District Consumer Commission Aurangabad C.C.No.336/2021 dated 26.07.2022): Jayashree w/o Satish Kharote v. The Oriental Insurance Company Ltd. & MD India Health Insurance TPA (ex parte). Insured purchased Corona Rakshak Policy No. 182100/48/2021/4321 valid 22.09.2020–03.07.2021; premium Rs. 2,580/-. COVID-positive by RTPCR on 09.03.2021; hospitalized at MIT Boys Hostel COVID centre (declared by Aurangabad Municipal Corporation) from 09.03.2021 to 16.03.2021; claim submitted 19.03.2021; repudiated on 06.05.2021 citing clause 3.6 (hospital definition/exclusion). Key issue: whether municipal COVID centre qualifies as “hospital” under policy and whether hospitalization exceeded 72 hours as required by clause 2 (operative clause) and clause 4.1 (COVID cover). Commission applied clause 3.6(vi): “any other set-up designated by the government as hospital for the treatment of Covid-19 shall also be considered as hospital,” held repudiation was deficiency in service, and quashed the District Commission dismissal. Final relief: insurer to pay lump sum Rs. 2,50,000/- with 8% p.a. interest from 06.05.2021 till realization plus Rs. 10,000/- for mental agony/harassment/litigation; no costs. Jayashree w/o Satish Kharote (insured/complainant) Rs. 2,50,000/- 8% p.a. from 06.05.2021 (date of repudiation) till realization Additionally, Rs. 10,000/- awarded for mental agony, financial harassment and cost of litigation. Insurance policy interpretation based on express definition of “hospital” including government-designated COVID-19 treatment set-ups (clause 3.6(vi)) Eligibility under Corona Rakshak Policy: COVID positive diagnosis from government-authorized diagnostic centre and minimum 72 hours continuous hospitalization (clauses 2 and 4.1) Repudiation contrary to policy terms amounts to deficiency in service under consumer law Quashing of District Commission order for failure to appreciate material evidence and policy clauses
